topic: Someone finds out their best friend is moving across the country
raw_output:
[story 1]

I was sorting through Liam’s old college textbooks, a dusty archaeological dig of shared memories, when I stumbled upon the envelope. It was addressed to a new address, a place so far away it felt like a different planet. My fingers, clumsy with sudden shock, traced the handwriting—his familiar, looping script, but overlaid with a new, unfamiliar urgency. My breath hitched, lodging somewhere tight in my chest. I reread the lines three times, the ink blurring slightly under the intensity of my gaze. When I finally looked up, the sunlight slanting through the window seemed too bright, the air suddenly thin. I dropped the papers, the sound barely audible in the quiet apartment. I walked to the window, staring out at the familiar street, feeling a strange, hollow pressure behind my ribs. How could this be? Liam, who shared my deepest secrets and my most mundane anxieties, was relocating to California? My mouth went dry. I needed a moment, just to process the sheer scale of the change.

[story 2]

"You're actually leaving?" The words sounded foreign, scraping against my tongue. I stared at the text message glowing on my phone screen, the confirmation of his move stark and undeniable. My hands started shaking, a rapid, uncontrolled tremor that I tried to suppress by gripping the edge of the desk until my knuckles turned white. I leaned back in my chair, the worn fabric protesting under the sudden tension in my posture. Every detail of the announcement—the dates, the packed boxes, the mention of a new city—seemed to swim before my eyes. A slow, heavy wave washed over me, making my vision swim momentarily. I pushed my chair back abruptly, the scrape echoing loudly in the room. I needed air. I walked to the kitchen, gripping the countertop, feeling a strange, dizzying disconnect from reality. This wasn't just a change of address; this was a seismic shift in the landscape of my life.

[story 3]

I found the news tucked inside a borrowed library book, the paper feeling brittle and fragile in my hands. Reading the final paragraph, a cold certainty settled over me, chilling me to the bone. My heart began to hammer against my ribs, a frantic drumbeat drowning out all other sound. I reread the sentence, focusing intently on the geography mentioned—the distance, the time zones. A strange tightness formed in my throat, making it difficult to draw a full breath. I stood up, my legs feeling unsteady, like they were disconnected from my core. I paced the small living room, my movements jerky and undirected. I stopped by the bookshelf, running my hand over the spines of books, searching for some anchor in the familiar. The reality of it—my best friend, physically relocating across the country—was too much to hold onto right now.
